Encryption of method calls — protocols sign each invocation with a per-class HMAC for security
Automatic test generation — pytest discovers protocols and creates test stubs for each defined method
questions.correctAnswer: Structural typing — any class with the right methods satisfies the protocol without explicit inheritance
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: third-party readers from any team satisfy DataReader by having .read(path) — no inheritance needed. Like Go interfaces, not Java interfaces.

questions.question: When should you use an ABC instead of a Protocol?
options: When the base class provides shared concrete behavior (logging, timing) that subclasses inherit
When the team has more than 5 implementations because Protocols cap at 5 conforming classes
When the framework integrates with Java code because Protocols are not visible to JVM bridges
When performance matters because ABC method dispatch is faster than Protocol attribute lookup
questions.correctAnswer: When the base class provides shared concrete behavior (logging, timing) that subclasses inherit
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: ABC + concrete shared methods (BaseTransformer with logging/timing). ABCs with only abstract methods = refactor to Protocol. CelticWind enforces this in review.

questions.question: What does `ValidatingReader(reader, validator)` demonstrate as a pattern?
options: Decorator — wraps a reader and adds validation behavior without modifying the original reader's class
Inheritance — extends CsvReader by overriding its read method with validation-aware logic
Singleton — ensures only one validator exists per pipeline instance through class-level locking
Facade — hides the reader's complexity behind a simpler API that the pipeline orchestration calls
questions.correctAnswer: Decorator — wraps a reader and adds validation behavior without modifying the original reader's class
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: composition + delegation. LoggingReader(ValidatingReader(RetryingReader(CsvReader()))) stacks behaviors. Same pattern as Python's @decorator syntax for functions.

questions.question: Why does CelticWind use manual constructor injection instead of a DI framework?
options: The framework's wiring is handled by a factory function — Python does not need Spring/Autofac
Constructor injection is required by Apache PEP 8 for any class with three or more dependencies
DI frameworks are forbidden by Lithuanian regulations because they obscure the dependency graph
Python's import system blocks DI frameworks from inspecting class hierarchies at runtime correctly
questions.correctAnswer: The framework's wiring is handled by a factory function — Python does not need Spring/Autofac
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: DI in Python = pass deps as __init__ params. Factory function reads config, instantiates, passes to SilverPipeline. No framework needed.

questions.question: What does the Strategy pattern configure that a parameter does not?
options: The behavior itself — StrictValidator rejects, LenientValidator warns; not just a value
The class hierarchy — strategies replace inheritance with delegation through a registry pattern
The async runtime — strategies run in their own event loop independently of the main pipeline
The serialization format — strategies are encoded in YAML while parameters are encoded in JSON
questions.correctAnswer: The behavior itself — StrictValidator rejects, LenientValidator warns; not just a value
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: parameter = value (max_kw=5000). Strategy = behavior (reject vs warn). New behavior = new class, no modification to BatchValidator.

questions.question: When should the Repository pattern be adopted at CelticWind?
options: When you have two or more concrete implementations — not when you have one and a theoretical future need
Always — the Repository pattern is required by every data engineering style guide for production code
Never — Python's pathlib and pyarrow already abstract storage, making repositories redundant overhead
On day one — abstract every storage interface preemptively because adding it later requires rewrites
questions.correctAnswer: When you have two or more concrete implementations — not when you have one and a theoretical future need
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: indirection costs complexity. CelticWind adopted Repository when adding Azure Blob — not preemptively. Two implementations = abstract; one = simpler direct call.

questions.question: What test distinguishes inheritance from composition in CelticWind's data models?
options: 'Can I pass this child object anywhere the parent is expected?' Yes = inheritance, no = composition
'How many fields does the child add?' More than 5 = composition, fewer = inheritance always
'Is the child class frozen?' frozen=True = composition, frozen=False = inheritance is correct
'Does the child override any methods?' Override = inheritance, no override = composition fits
questions.correctAnswer: 'Can I pass this child object anywhere the parent is expected?' Yes = inheritance, no = composition
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: EnrichedReading IS-A ValidatedReading (inheritance). PipelineRunResult HAS RegionalSummaries (composition). Liskov substitution test guides every model.

questions.question: When should you use generic types `Generic[T]` versus concrete types?
options: Generics for framework components; concrete types for business logic that handles a specific domain
Generics for everything — concrete types are deprecated in modern Python and break mypy strict mode
Generics for performance-critical code — they enable JIT compilation that concrete types skip entirely
Generics for asyncio code only — synchronous code uses concrete types per Python's threading guidelines
questions.correctAnswer: Generics for framework components; concrete types for business logic that handles a specific domain
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: PipelineStep[T, U] = framework. validate_meter_readings(list[BaseMeterReading]) = business logic. Over-genericizing business code adds abstraction without value.

questions.question: What does Module 1's architecture enable for Module 2's concurrency?
options: Concurrency requires no architectural changes — a ConcurrentCsvReader satisfies the same DataReader protocol
Concurrency requires rewriting every component because protocols cannot express async semantics correctly
Concurrency replaces dependency injection — concurrent pipelines build their dependencies inline at runtime
Concurrency requires forking the framework — sequential and concurrent pipelines cannot share a codebase
questions.correctAnswer: Concurrency requires no architectural changes — a ConcurrentCsvReader satisfies the same DataReader protocol
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: protocols don't specify sequential vs concurrent. Pipeline gets concurrent reader through DI without orchestration changes. Architecture enables concurrency.

questions.question: Why is the instinct 'parallelize everything' wrong for the CelticWind pipeline?
options: The 12-second write step is already saturating disk bandwidth; threading would not make it faster
Python's GIL forbids any parallelism so concurrent code provides exactly zero speedup over sequential
Multiprocessing is licensed per worker by Apache and CelticWind cannot afford more than one worker
Lithuanian regulations forbid concurrent processing of meter data for cybersecurity compliance reasons
questions.correctAnswer: The 12-second write step is already saturating disk bandwidth; threading would not make it faster
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: profile first, identify bottleneck, apply concurrency only where it crosses the benefit threshold. Some steps are not worth parallelizing.

questions.question: What is the GIL's effect on threading vs multiprocessing?
options: Threading: GIL released during I/O so threads run truly concurrently. CPU-bound: GIL held, threads take turns
Threading: GIL replaced by per-thread locks on every CPython 3.12+ build by default automatically
Threading: GIL applies only to async code — synchronous threads bypass the GIL and run in parallel
Threading: GIL affects only multiprocessing — single-threaded code runs without any GIL involvement
questions.correctAnswer: Threading: GIL released during I/O so threads run truly concurrently. CPU-bound: GIL held, threads take turns
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: GIL released during I/O = threading helps. GIL held during Python bytecode = threading does NOT help CPU-bound. Multiprocessing has separate GILs per process.

questions.question: Why is the `with ThreadPoolExecutor() as pool:` context manager required at CelticWind?
options: Ensures the pool is shut down cleanly after all tasks — even if an exception occurs in the body
Improves performance — context-managed pools cache thread state across the program's lifetime
Required by Python's typing system — ThreadPoolExecutor cannot be used outside a `with` block
Encrypts inter-thread communication — without it, threads can read each other's memory unsafely
questions.correctAnswer: Ensures the pool is shut down cleanly after all tasks — even if an exception occurs in the body
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: forgotten shutdown() leaves worker threads running, consuming resources, holding file locks. Context manager guarantees cleanup.

questions.question: What does `Semaphore(max_concurrent)` do in concurrent API calls?
options: Limits how many threads can hold the semaphore at once — caps concurrent in-flight API requests
Synchronizes thread output by serializing every print and log call through one shared lock
Encrypts payload between threads with a session key derived from the semaphore's hash value
Distributes load across multiple cloud regions by routing requests through a region-locked queue
questions.correctAnswer: Limits how many threads can hold the semaphore at once — caps concurrent in-flight API requests
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: 8 threads start API calls simultaneously without it = burst exceeds rate limit. Semaphore caps concurrency; sleep paces calls.

questions.question: Why must `validate_chunk` be a top-level function (not nested or a lambda) for `ProcessPoolExecutor`?
options: Worker processes pickle the function to communicate it — pickle requires module-level functions
Top-level functions run faster because Python caches their bytecode at the module's import time
Worker processes share top-level functions through Linux's fork mechanism for memory efficiency
Top-level functions are required by mypy when used inside ProcessPoolExecutor for type checking
questions.correctAnswer: Worker processes pickle the function to communicate it — pickle requires module-level functions
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: ProcessPoolExecutor pickles the function across the process boundary. Lambdas, nested funcs, methods are unpicklable.

questions.question: When does `asyncio` outperform `ThreadPoolExecutor` for I/O-bound work?
options: When the concurrency count is high (hundreds of API calls) — coroutines are lighter than threads
When the workload is CPU-bound — asyncio bypasses the GIL by running coroutines on separate cores
When the network is unreliable — asyncio retries automatically while threads require manual logic
When the I/O is local — asyncio has special filesystem support that threading lacks entirely
questions.correctAnswer: When the concurrency count is high (hundreds of API calls) — coroutines are lighter than threads
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: 365 concurrent API calls = asyncio's lighter coroutines beat thread pool. Threading is fine up to ~20 concurrent; asyncio scales to thousands.

questions.question: What does `asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True)` change?
options: Exceptions become return values instead of cancelling all sibling tasks — partial success is collected
Exceptions are logged with full stack traces while gather still raises the first exception found
Exceptions are encrypted in the result list to prevent sensitive error messages from leaking
Exceptions trigger automatic retries with exponential backoff before the gather call completes
questions.correctAnswer: Exceptions become return values instead of cancelling all sibling tasks — partial success is collected
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: default = first exception cancels all. return_exceptions=True = collect successes + failures. Filter list comprehension on the result.

questions.question: How does the concurrency model decision flow at CelticWind?
options: I/O-bound + < 20 tasks → threading; I/O-bound + many tasks → asyncio; CPU-bound > 60s → multiprocessing
Always asyncio for everything because asyncio is the modern Python concurrency standard since 3.10
Always multiprocessing because multiple cores are always available and provide guaranteed speedup
Always sequential because concurrency overhead exceeds the benefits at any pipeline scale
questions.correctAnswer: I/O-bound + < 20 tasks → threading; I/O-bound + many tasks → asyncio; CPU-bound > 60s → multiprocessing
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: I/O fits threading or asyncio (by count); CPU > 60s threshold for multiprocessing. Architecture stays the same; concurrency is targeted to bottlenecks.

questions.question: Why does CelticWind treat sequential as the default and require justification for concurrency?
options: 27 minutes of SLA headroom is a maintenance advantage — every line of sequential code is debuggable by anyone
Concurrency is forbidden by the engineering style guide because of past incidents involving thread races
Concurrent code is 10× slower in Python due to GIL contention so sequential is always the best choice
Sequential code uses less RAM than concurrent code regardless of workload characteristics every time
questions.correctAnswer: 27 minutes of SLA headroom is a maintenance advantage — every line of sequential code is debuggable by anyone
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: 3-min pipeline within 30-min SLA = not a perf problem. Headroom = safety margin for growth. Saving 2 min costs maintainability.

questions.question: Why does the concurrent pipeline make Module 3's memory problem worse?
options: ThreadPoolExecutor reads 8 files concurrently — 8 lists merged into one 2.4 GB list before validation
ThreadPoolExecutor disables Python's garbage collector to avoid race conditions during file reads
ThreadPoolExecutor caches file contents in shared memory so they cannot be released between runs
ThreadPoolExecutor doubles memory because each thread maintains a private copy of the entire dataset
questions.correctAnswer: ThreadPoolExecutor reads 8 files concurrently — 8 lists merged into one 2.4 GB list before validation
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: 8 concurrent reads → 8 lists → 1 merged list = 2.4 GB peak. Module 3 teaches chunking, Polars, and pyarrow to fix this.

questions.question: Why does Module 3 say memory becomes a budget at mid-level scale?
options: 1.2M rows = 2,400 MB list[dict] + 1,200 MB validate + 1,200 MB transform = 4,800 MB peak crash
1.2M rows trigger Lithuania's data sovereignty law that caps in-memory record counts globally
1.2M rows are blocked by Python's interpreter limit — defaults reject above 1M element lists
1.2M rows require an Apache Spark license fee that scales linearly with row count per pipeline run
questions.correctAnswer: 1.2M rows = 2,400 MB list[dict] + 1,200 MB validate + 1,200 MB transform = 4,800 MB peak crash
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: junior scale = memory free. Mid scale = 'load and process' becomes 'chunk and stream'. Defining architectural change of mid-level Python.

questions.question: What is the right mental model for `del` in pipeline code?
options: Use for objects > 100 MB no longer needed — not for small objects where GC handles cleanup efficiently
Use for every variable after every line — explicit del is required by Python's strict mode in 3.13+
Use only inside try blocks — del outside try-except is unsafe and can corrupt the interpreter state
Use only on lists — del on dicts and sets raises TypeError because they have a different lifecycle
questions.correctAnswer: Use for objects > 100 MB no longer needed — not for small objects where GC handles cleanup efficiently
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: explicit del before the next allocation. Don't sprinkle del everywhere for small objects — adds noise without benefit. > 100 MB threshold at CelticWind.

questions.question: What is the key advantage of the chunked pipeline pattern?
options: Constant memory usage — chunk_size × 2 KB regardless of total row count — small objects are GC'd per iter
Faster execution — chunks complete in parallel through Python's threading module without any extra code
Strong guarantees — the database transaction wraps each chunk so partial failures cannot corrupt output
Higher throughput — chunked I/O bypasses the file system cache and reads directly from the disk
questions.correctAnswer: Constant memory usage — chunk_size × 2 KB regardless of total row count — small objects are GC'd per iter
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: yield → process → write per chunk. Memory stays constant. Garbage collected at end of each iteration. Aggregations need a separate merge step.

questions.question: Why does Polars use ~6× less memory than `list[dict]` for the same data?
options: Columnar storage — each column stored once with type info, not per-row dict overhead per record
Compression — Polars compresses every column with snappy by default to reduce memory footprint
Lazy materialization — Polars only loads columns into memory as they are accessed in expressions
Memory-mapped files — Polars maps the source file directly without copying any data into RAM
questions.correctAnswer: Columnar storage — each column stored once with type info, not per-row dict overhead per record
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: 1.2M dicts (each with 5 string keys) = 2,400 MB. Polars columnar = 380 MB. Same data, different layout.

questions.question: What is CelticWind's rule about converting between `list[dict]` and Polars DataFrames?
options: Convert once at the boundary — stay in Polars for analytical steps; conversion costs ~1s per million rows
Convert on every step — interoperability is more important than the per-conversion overhead
Convert never — pick one representation and use it throughout the entire pipeline forever
Convert only at runtime — type checkers complain about Polars types so build-time stays in dicts
questions.correctAnswer: Convert once at the boundary — stay in Polars for analytical steps; conversion costs ~1s per million rows
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: 0.3s aggregation + 2s round-trip conversion = no benefit. Convert at boundary (after validation), stay in Polars (write_parquet directly).

questions.question: What does Polars' lazy evaluation enable?
options: Build a query plan, optimize, execute in one pass — declarative thinking instead of imperative
Cache intermediate DataFrames in Redis so repeated queries return instantly across runs always
Run computations in a separate thread that the GIL cannot block during the lazy evaluation step
Defer all errors until collect() is called so the pipeline can ignore broken steps in development
questions.correctAnswer: Build a query plan, optimize, execute in one pass — declarative thinking instead of imperative
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: scan_csv + filter + groupby + agg → optimizer applies all in one pass. Same model as PySpark/Spark SQL/dbt — declarative thinking.

questions.question: What is the most common pyarrow performance mistake?
options: Converting to list[dict] for processing and back to pyarrow for writing — each conversion copies the data
Reading all columns instead of using projection pushdown to read only the columns the pipeline needs
Compressing with snappy instead of zstd — zstd has better ratios for analytical workloads at scale
Storing pyarrow Tables in Redis instead of using the local filesystem for intermediate persistence
questions.correctAnswer: Converting to list[dict] for processing and back to pyarrow for writing — each conversion copies the data
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: each conversion copies the entire dataset. Use Polars (Arrow-native) or pyarrow.compute. Convert to list[dict] only for per-row Pydantic/API logic.

questions.question: Why does CelticWind disable tracemalloc in production?
options: Overhead pushes a 95%-capacity pipeline over the edge — production uses resource.getrusage instead
Production has no shell access — tracemalloc cannot dump its output without an interactive terminal
Production runs as root — tracemalloc requires non-root user and refuses to start in the production pod
Production uses Python 3.12 — tracemalloc was removed in 3.12 in favor of memray for memory profiling
questions.correctAnswer: Overhead pushes a 95%-capacity pipeline over the edge — production uses resource.getrusage instead
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: tracemalloc tracks every alloc → CPU + memory overhead. resource.getrusage is OS-level with zero overhead. Enable tracemalloc only during incident investigation.

questions.question: What is the 80/20 insight about memory optimization?
options: 80% of memory comes from 20% of code — typically initial load + 1-2 large intermediates. Optimize those 3 lines.
80% of memory savings come from 20% of optimizations — top 5 patterns deliver 80% of total savings overall
80% of pipelines have memory issues — 20% of teams cause 80% of out-of-memory incidents at production scale
80% of memory is wasted by Python's GC — turning off automatic GC saves 80% of total runtime memory peak
questions.correctAnswer: 80% of memory comes from 20% of code — typically initial load + 1-2 large intermediates. Optimize those 3 lines.
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: 3 alloc sites = 92% of CelticWind's peak memory. Optimizing those reduced 2.4 GB → 185 MB. 80/20 applies to memory like everything else.

questions.question: What does the memory-efficient pipeline architecture look like at CelticWind?
options: CSV+Pydantic path: chunked dicts. SCADA Parquet path: pyarrow + zero-copy Polars throughout the stage
All paths: load full dataset + apply Polars on the entire frame to maximize Polars vectorization gains
All paths: stream JSON line by line through asyncio so memory is constant across every pipeline stage
All paths: store everything in S3 and stream from S3 on demand to bypass local memory entirely
questions.correctAnswer: CSV+Pydantic path: chunked dicts. SCADA Parquet path: pyarrow + zero-copy Polars throughout the stage
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: per-row needs (Pydantic) = chunked dicts. Analytical needs (aggregation) = Polars. Both stay within 2 GB on 4 GB server.

questions.question: When did CelticWind decide to build a framework?
options: When the 8th pipeline was added and more than half of new code was copy-pasted from existing pipelines
On day one — every team should build a framework before writing any production code at all
After hiring 50 engineers — the framework decision is dictated by team size not by technical needs
After Apache published the official Python pipeline framework specification version 2.0 last year
questions.correctAnswer: When the 8th pipeline was added and more than half of new code was copy-pasted from existing pipelines
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: 3 pipelines = 3 scripts is simpler. > half copy-pasted = framework justified. Below the threshold, abstraction overhead exceeds duplication cost.

questions.question: What design principle does the registry pattern implement?
options: Open/Closed — open for extension (register new components), closed for modification (no framework changes)
Single Responsibility — each registry handles exactly one component type's lifecycle and creation
Liskov Substitution — registries guarantee that any registered component is interchangeable safely
Interface Segregation — registries split monolithic interfaces into smaller per-component contracts
questions.correctAnswer: Open/Closed — open for extension (register new components), closed for modification (no framework changes)
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: chain of if/elif grows with each new component, requires modifying framework code. Registry = dict lookup, external code extends without framework changes.

questions.question: What does Python's entry-point mechanism enable for CelticWind's framework?
options: Per-team plugin packages discover automatically at startup — framework never imports team packages directly
Per-team plugins compile to native code through Cython for performance gains across the entire system
Per-team plugins encrypt their entry points with team-specific keys for cross-team isolation guarantees
Per-team plugins replace pyproject.toml because entry points define everything Python packages need
questions.correctAnswer: Per-team plugin packages discover automatically at startup — framework never imports team packages directly
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: same mechanism as pytest plugins, Flask extensions. New team installs their package → entry points auto-register their components. Framework stays unaware.

questions.question: What does the topological sort on `StepDefinition.depends_on` produce?
options: Execution layers — steps in the same layer can run concurrently; layers run sequentially across the DAG
Linear order — every step gets a number from 1 to N and runs strictly in numbered sequence forever
Random order — the sort shuffles to load-balance across workers regardless of dependencies declared
Reverse order — leaves first, roots last so the framework finalizes outputs before reading inputs
questions.correctAnswer: Execution layers — steps in the same layer can run concurrently; layers run sequentially across the DAG
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: layer 0 = no deps (read_meters, read_prices, read_weather → concurrent). Layer N = depends on all earlier. Same model as Apache Airflow at smaller scale.

questions.question: What separation does the StepHook pattern preserve?
options: Business logic in components (RegionNormalizer normalizes); infrastructure in hooks (logging, metrics, retry)
Synchronous logic in components; asynchronous logic in hooks for latency-sensitive operations only
Public API in components; private implementation in hooks invisible to the consuming team's code
CPU work in components; I/O work in hooks because hooks have separate threading semantics globally
questions.correctAnswer: Business logic in components (RegionNormalizer normalizes); infrastructure in hooks (logging, metrics, retry)
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: change logging format = update one hook, not 12 components. Components stay focused on their transformation logic; hooks add infrastructure transparently.

questions.question: What does CelticWind's rule about YAML configuration forbid?
options: Conditional logic, loops, or inline Python — YAML declares which components and how they are configured
More than 50 lines per pipeline — YAML files exceeding this limit are split into included files automatically
Comments — YAML pipelines must be self-documenting and any explanation belongs in a separate Markdown
Component versions — pinning versions in YAML breaks the registry's auto-upgrade mechanism completely
questions.correctAnswer: Conditional logic, loops, or inline Python — YAML declares which components and how they are configured
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: logic in YAML = meta-programming language without debugger/types/tests. Conditional behavior = a RoutingValidator component, not a YAML if-statement.

questions.question: What does a SchemaContract between pipeline steps prevent?
options: Implicit interface — steps document their required/produced columns and the framework verifies them
Schema evolution — contracts freeze the schema permanently so no producer can ever add a column
Type errors — contracts validate Python types at runtime through Pydantic before each step runs
Performance regressions — contracts measure execution time and reject any step that slows below SLA
questions.correctAnswer: Implicit interface — steps document their required/produced columns and the framework verifies them
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: without contracts, you read the producer's code to know its outputs. Contracts make interface explicit and verifiable at build time.

questions.question: What does the `skip` recovery strategy require to be safe?
options: The step must be truly optional — its absence does not silently corrupt downstream results
The step must complete in under 60 seconds — longer steps cannot recover from a skip cleanly
The step must run on a separate worker — skipping affects only the worker that executed the step
The step must be implemented in Cython — Python steps cannot be skipped due to interpreter limits
questions.correctAnswer: The step must be truly optional — its absence does not silently corrupt downstream results
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: weather enrichment skip = safe (billing optional). Validation skip = unvalidated data hits Gold (dangerous). CelticWind's YAML requires comment explaining why optional.

questions.question: What is the right progression toward a framework at CelticWind?
options: Shared utilities (months 1-3) → registries + factory (months 4-9) → full framework with DAG + hooks (month 10+)
Full framework on day 1 — never write any pipeline code without the framework's structure first
Full framework on day 30 — after the team has 30 days of pipeline experience to inform design
Never frameworks — every pipeline should be standalone for maximum independence between teams
questions.correctAnswer: Shared utilities (months 1-3) → registries + factory (months 4-9) → full framework with DAG + hooks (month 10+)
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: each step motivated by real pain, not anticipated need. Partial framework served for 6 months before the full version was built.

questions.question: What did CelticWind's framework deliver in code metrics?
options: 12 pipelines: 8,000 LOC (60% duplicated) → 12 YAML + 3,200 LOC shared
12 pipelines: 8,000 LOC → 800 LOC by removing all comments and using minified Python syntax
12 pipelines: 8,000 LOC → 0 LOC because the framework auto-generates all pipeline implementations
12 pipelines: 8,000 LOC → 24,000 LOC because the framework adds substantial abstraction overhead
questions.correctAnswer: 12 pipelines: 8,000 LOC (60% duplicated) → 12 YAML + 3,200 LOC shared
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: 12 YAML files + 3,200 LOC of shared code. Module 5 then layers in advanced configuration management on top of these YAML pipeline definitions.

questions.question: Why is configuration treated as infrastructure at mid-level scale?
options: A config error has the same impact as a code bug — wrong output, data loss, or pipeline failure
A config error is automatically fixed by Python's interpreter through default value substitution
A config error is detected by the regulator's automated audit before the pipeline can deploy live
A config error is harmless — the pipeline runs with whatever values are present in the YAML files
questions.correctAnswer: A config error has the same impact as a code bug — wrong output, data loss, or pipeline failure
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: junior = convenience. Mid = infrastructure. Configuration deserves the same engineering rigor as code: typed, validated, tested, versioned.

questions.question: What does pydantic-settings' `Field(ge=1000, le=10000)` provide?
options: Validation at load time — `max_daily_kw=500` raises ValidationError immediately, not at runtime
Encryption at rest — values outside the range are stored encrypted in the YAML file by default
Auto-correction — values outside the range are clamped to the nearest valid bound silently
Performance optimization — Python's interpreter caches values inside the range for faster access
questions.correctAnswer: Validation at load time — `max_daily_kw=500` raises ValidationError immediately, not at runtime
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: declarative validation. max_daily_kw=500 raises 'should be >= 1000' at load. Model class = both validation logic AND schema documentation.

questions.question: What is the configuration source precedence order in pydantic-settings?
options: CLI args > env vars > .env file > YAML > model defaults — highest priority wins
Model defaults > YAML > .env file > env vars > CLI args — most stable values win
Whichever is loaded first wins — order depends on the application's import sequence at startup
Random precedence — pydantic-settings shuffles sources to prevent staleness in cached values
questions.correctAnswer: CLI args > env vars > .env file > YAML > model defaults — highest priority wins
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: CLI > env vars > .env > YAML > defaults. Stale CELTICWIND_LOG_LEVEL=DEBUG silently overrides YAML INFO. Log effective config at startup to surface overrides.

questions.question: Why is `CELTICWIND_ENV` set by infrastructure (Kubernetes/Docker) and not by the application?
options: Application cannot accidentally override its own environment detection — infrastructure controls cannot be bypassed
Applications run faster when environment variables are set externally rather than through Python code
Lithuanian regulations require infrastructure-level environment management for all production pipelines
Python's os.environ has a 32-character limit per variable that infrastructure can override per pod
questions.correctAnswer: Application cannot accidentally override its own environment detection — infrastructure controls cannot be bypassed
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: production K8s pod spec sets CELTICWIND_ENV=production. App trusts it absolutely. Missing = development (safe fallback).

questions.question: What is CelticWind's hierarchy of credential management?
options: Eliminate (IAM) → Generate dynamically (OAuth tokens) → Store in Vault (static secrets)
Hardcode → DB → Vault → Encrypted env vars — progressive security from least to most
Per-task tokens → Cached in Redis → Replicated across workers → Synced to metadata DB always
Static passwords → Rotated quarterly → Stored in metadata DB → Encrypted with team key globally
questions.correctAnswer: Eliminate (IAM) → Generate dynamically (OAuth tokens) → Store in Vault (static secrets)
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: zero credentials > per-task tokens > stored secrets. EnvironmentSecretsProvider for dev, AzureKeyVaultProvider for prod. Same protocol.

questions.question: Why does CelticWind put feature flags on a removal date?
options: Flags permanently enabled = dead code — conditional logic with one path never exercised again
Flags consume memory in Python's interpreter cache and slow startup if too many accumulate
Flags are billed per-flag by the configuration management vendor on a monthly subscription basis
Flags must be removed by the regulator's quarterly audit deadline or the audit fails
questions.correctAnswer: Flags permanently enabled = dead code — conditional logic with one path never exercised again
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: stale flag = technical debt. After 30 days stable, remove flag and simplify the conditional. Code review checklist asks about flags > 90 days old.

questions.question: What is the cost-benefit of configuration tests?
options: Seconds to write, milliseconds to run, prevents hours of production downtime — among the best ROI
Hours to write, seconds to run, prevents minor inconveniences — moderate ROI for the engineering effort
Days to write, minutes to run, required by Lithuanian regulators — compliance-driven, not value-driven
Weeks to write, hours to run, replaces the need for any other testing — high effort but maximum coverage
questions.correctAnswer: Seconds to write, milliseconds to run, prevents hours of production downtime — among the best ROI
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: parametrize over all envs, assert constraints. CelticWind: 4 prevented incidents in 6 months, each would have cost 2+ hours of engineer time.

questions.question: What does configuration drift detection enable?
options: Compare active config hash to expected hash — warn when production differs from the committed YAML
Block any deployment with drift — refuse to start the pipeline until the YAML is back in sync
Auto-correct drift by overwriting the production config with the latest YAML from the main branch
Encrypt the drift signal — only the security team can read the warning about configuration drift
questions.correctAnswer: Compare active config hash to expected hash — warn when production differs from the committed YAML
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: drift warning, not block — emergency overrides are valid. Policy: investigate within 48 hours, intentional drift updates the committed YAML.

questions.question: What is the CelticWind rule for what to make configurable?
options: Configurable when the value changes between environments; in code when the same in every environment
Configurable for everything — make every constant a YAML setting for maximum flexibility always
Configurable for nothing — hardcode all values in code so the pipeline behavior is fully deterministic
Configurable only for paths — every other value belongs in code regardless of environment differences
questions.correctAnswer: Configurable when the value changes between environments; in code when the same in every environment
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: 22 settings in CelticWind's PipelineConfig. Periodic review removes settings with same value in every env — those are defaults that belong in code.

questions.question: How does Module 6's testing layer build on Module 5's configuration layer?
options: Property tests generate random valid/invalid configs; integration tests use development profile + CLI overrides
Property tests replace configuration tests entirely because Hypothesis generates better test cases
Integration tests skip configuration because they run on the real production server with live secrets
Configuration tests are a subset of integration tests and Module 6 supersedes Module 5 entirely
questions.correctAnswer: Property tests generate random valid/invalid configs; integration tests use development profile + CLI overrides
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: Module 5 typed config makes integration testing practical. Hypothesis generates configs for validation, integration tests use dev profile + overrides.

questions.question: What is the testing mindset shift between junior and mid level?
options: From 'right answer for these inputs?' to 'correct for any valid input + consistent output composed?'
From writing tests by hand to having pytest auto-generate tests from function signatures and types
From local testing to remote testing on Apache's centralized test execution platform globally
From English test names to Esperanto test names because Esperanto names sort alphabetically better
questions.correctAnswer: From 'right answer for these inputs?' to 'correct for any valid input + consistent output composed?'
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: example-based (junior) → property-based + snapshot + integration (mid). Verify invariants, detect changes, verify component interactions.

questions.question: What does property-based testing verify with Hypothesis?
options: Universal properties (output count = input count, valid records meet constraints) across random inputs
Specific examples — Hypothesis is just a fancier @parametrize decorator with the same example-based mindset
Performance characteristics — Hypothesis benchmarks each function on 10,000 random inputs by default
Type correctness — Hypothesis runs mypy in strict mode on every test function before execution
questions.correctAnswer: Universal properties (output count = input count, valid records meet constraints) across random inputs
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: Hypothesis generates 200+ random inputs and verifies invariants hold. Complementary to example tests, not a replacement for them.

questions.question: What is the value of `@st.composite` custom strategies?
options: Generate domain-specific data with constraints — meter_id_strategy matches IE-XXX-X-NNNNN exactly
Compose strategies from primitives — the decorator combines st.integers and st.floats into product types
Run strategies in parallel — composite strategies generate inputs across multiple cores at once safely
Cache strategies in Redis — composite strategies persist across test runs for faster CI execution
questions.correctAnswer: Generate domain-specific data with constraints — meter_id_strategy matches IE-XXX-X-NNNNN exactly
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: realistic data generators replace handcrafted fixtures. Hypothesis shrinks failures to minimal examples — points to root cause, not just one symptom.

questions.question: What is the most common snapshot test mistake?
options: Including dynamic fields (timestamp, UUID, duration) without normalizing — fails on every run
Reading the snapshot from disk instead of from Redis — disk reads are slower and break CI parallelism
Comparing JSON strings instead of dictionaries — JSON ordering varies between Python versions
Storing snapshots in Git — binary files belong in S3 storage and are never committed to version control
questions.correctAnswer: Including dynamic fields (timestamp, UUID, duration) without normalizing — fails on every run
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: dynamic fields → false positives. Normalize timestamp='NORMALIZED', duration=0.0 before comparison. Maintain normalize function alongside the snapshot.

questions.question: What does the `@pytest.mark.integration` marker enable at CelticWind?
options: PR check runs unit tests (2s); merge runs integration tests (8s) — fast feedback for developers
Integration tests run on a separate machine with production-like resources for accurate results
Integration tests share fixtures with unit tests but use real databases instead of mocked ones
Integration tests are auto-skipped on Tuesdays per the team's reduced-load development schedule
questions.correctAnswer: PR check runs unit tests (2s); merge runs integration tests (8s) — fast feedback for developers
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: PR fast feedback (unit only). Merge runs full suite (incl integration). Local: pytest -m 'not integration' for fast iteration.

questions.question: When should you use a factory versus a Hypothesis strategy?
options: Factory for example-based tests asserting on specific values; strategy for property-based tests asserting invariants
Factory for unit tests; strategy for integration tests — these are completely different testing layers
Factory for fixtures shared across tests; strategy for one-off test inputs that change every run
Factory for synchronous tests; strategy for async tests because Hypothesis only supports asyncio
questions.correctAnswer: Factory for example-based tests asserting on specific values; strategy for property-based tests asserting invariants
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: factory_meter_readings(count=20, region='DUBLIN') = specific assertion. valid_meter_reading() Hypothesis = property over any valid reading. Both replace fixtures.

questions.question: Why does CelticWind avoid timing assertions in unit tests?
options: Slow CI servers, heavy load, small data — timing tests have false positives that train teams to retry
Timing assertions are forbidden by Apache PEP 8 because they introduce non-determinism into CI
Timing assertions require root permissions to install timing instrumentation in production-like environments
Timing assertions are 10× slower than functional tests so they exceed the CI budget for every PR check
questions.correctAnswer: Slow CI servers, heavy load, small data — timing tests have false positives that train teams to retry
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: timing for performance regression tests on dedicated hardware (lesson 8). Unit/integration tests assert correctness only — never speed.

questions.question: What is the difference between absolute and relative performance thresholds?
options: Absolute (< 5s) catches CI hardware regressions; relative (< 12× ratio) catches O(n²) algorithmic regressions
Absolute is required for unit tests; relative is required for integration tests in CelticWind's policy
Absolute uses milliseconds; relative uses microseconds — both measure exactly the same dimension
Absolute and relative are equivalent — choosing between them depends on the team's preferred style
questions.correctAnswer: Absolute (< 5s) catches CI hardware regressions; relative (< 12× ratio) catches O(n²) algorithmic regressions
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: absolute breaks when CI hardware changes. Relative tests algorithmic complexity (O(n) = 10× ratio, O(n²) = 100×). CelticWind uses both on critical paths.

questions.question: What does CelticWind's test marker organization enable?
options: PR: -m 'not slow' (260 tests, 10s); merge: full suite (280 tests, 70s); nightly: stress tests too
All tests run on every PR regardless of marker — markers are documentation only at CelticWind
Markers run tests across multiple cloud providers — each marker maps to a specific cloud region globally
Markers encrypt test results for the security team's audit log of all engineering test executions
questions.correctAnswer: PR: -m 'not slow' (260 tests, 10s); merge: full suite (280 tests, 70s); nightly: stress tests too
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: markers = unit/integration/slow/snapshot. PR runs fast subset; merge runs full; nightly runs stress. Treat test suite as a product.

questions.question: How does the CI/CD pipeline (Module 7) build on the test suite (Module 6)?
options: Without comprehensive tests, CI/CD = automated risk deployment — tests provide confidence to deploy automatically
CI/CD replaces tests entirely because automated deployment makes manual testing redundant for production
CI/CD runs tests only once at staging — production deploys without re-running tests for performance
CI/CD requires zero tests because the deployment system has its own validation layer at runtime
questions.correctAnswer: Without comprehensive tests, CI/CD = automated risk deployment — tests provide confidence to deploy automatically
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: 280-test suite → CI confidence → automatic deploy. Without tests, CI/CD just automates the risk. Module 7's pipeline depends on Module 6's tests.

questions.question: What does the CI/CD pipeline do beyond what tests + config systems provide?
options: Ensures correct, portable code reaches production reliably, reproducibly, and reversibly
Replaces the need for tests by relying on production telemetry to catch any bugs after deployment
Encrypts the deployment payload so production servers verify the cryptographic signature first
Runs the pipeline on the developer's laptop instead of the production server for cost efficiency
questions.correctAnswer: Ensures correct, portable code reaches production reliably, reproducibly, and reversibly
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: tests prove correctness, config makes portable, CI/CD ensures the correct + portable code reaches production. Manual deploy = weakest link.

questions.question: Why does CelticWind generate a lock file (pip freeze > requirements.lock) even though pyproject.toml has version ranges?
options: Ranges (>=2.5,<3.0) for flexibility; lock file (2.7.1 exactly) for identical staging+prod versions
Lock files are encrypted by Apache and required for any production package distribution worldwide
Lock files enable parallel pip install — without them, pip serializes installation across cores
Lock files are smaller than pyproject.toml — they reduce CI artifact sizes by an order of magnitude
questions.correctAnswer: Ranges (>=2.5,<3.0) for flexibility; lock file (2.7.1 exactly) for identical staging+prod versions
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: pyproject.toml = compatibility ranges. Lock file = exact reproducibility. CI generates lock, deployment uses it. Prevents 'requests 2.31 vs 2.32' surprises.

questions.question: What does semantic versioning (1.2.0 → 2.0.0) communicate?
options: MAJOR = breaking change (consumers must update); MINOR = new feature; PATCH = bug fix only
MAJOR = important release; MINOR = small release; PATCH = anything from typo fix to feature add
MAJOR = production deployment; MINOR = staging deployment; PATCH = development only — never deploy
MAJOR = annual release; MINOR = quarterly release; PATCH = monthly release — calendar-based
questions.correctAnswer: MAJOR = breaking change (consumers must update); MINOR = new feature; PATCH = bug fix only
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: MAJOR breaking, MINOR feature, PATCH fix. CI rejects builds reusing a published version number. New fix = new version (1.2.1 not 1.2.0 republished).

questions.question: Why must secrets never appear in a Dockerfile?
options: ENV CELTICWIND_KEY=... embeds the secret in every image layer — anyone who pulls can extract it
Dockerfile env vars are slower than runtime env vars by 10× because they go through extra parsing
Dockerfile env vars conflict with Kubernetes pod env vars and the pod spec wins silently always
Dockerfile env vars are encrypted with a per-build key that is lost when the build cache is cleared
questions.correctAnswer: ENV CELTICWIND_KEY=... embeds the secret in every image layer — anyone who pulls can extract it
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: docker history / docker inspect reveals embedded ENV. Inject at runtime via -e or K8s secrets. CI checks reject Dockerfiles with secret-prefix ENV lines.

questions.question: What does a multi-stage Dockerfile achieve?
options: Build stage has build tools (compilers, build); runtime stage has only the wheel — smaller, more secure
Build stage runs in parallel — multi-stage Dockerfiles auto-distribute across all available CPU cores
Build stage compiles to native code — multi-stage Dockerfiles produce binary executables not Python wheels
Build stage is encrypted — only the runtime stage is decrypted on the production server during boot
questions.correctAnswer: Build stage has build tools (compilers, build); runtime stage has only the wheel — smaller, more secure
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: stage 1 builds wheel with build tools. Stage 2 = python:slim + install wheel. Smaller image, no build tools in production.

questions.question: What does CelticWind's CI pipeline do on a tag push (`refs/tags/v*`)?
options: Builds the wheel + Docker image and pushes to the container registry for production deployment
Reverts to the previous tag — tag pushes trigger automatic rollback to the prior production version
Sends an email to all subscribers — tag pushes are subscription notifications without any deploy action
Locks the main branch — tag pushes prevent further commits until the tag is manually unlocked
questions.correctAnswer: Builds the wheel + Docker image and pushes to the container registry for production deployment
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: lint+test on every PR/push. build+publish only on main or tags. Production releases use tagged builds with predictable versions.

questions.question: Why does CelticWind set the false-positive target for CI gates at < 2%?
options: 30% false positives train developers to retry without investigation — quality gates lose meaning entirely
2% is an Apache-standard threshold required for any open-source project to receive Foundation funding
2% matches the regulatory ceiling for false alerts in any data engineering pipeline globally enforced
2% is the maximum that PagerDuty's alerting system can handle without rate-limiting downstream
questions.correctAnswer: 30% false positives train developers to retry without investigation — quality gates lose meaning entirely
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: > 2% false positives → developers learn to ignore. Flaky tests get fixed or quarantined. Network-dependent tests use mocks.

questions.question: Why does CelticWind use rolling deployment (not blue-green) for the billing pipeline?
options: Billing reads live API prices — Blue and Green produce different output from the same input timing
Billing data is too large for blue-green — staging cannot duplicate the production data volume cheaply
Billing has stricter SLAs than blue-green can guarantee due to its 30-second comparison overhead
Billing uses a different Python interpreter — blue-green requires identical interpreters in both slots
questions.correctAnswer: Billing reads live API prices — Blue and Green produce different output from the same input timing
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: blue-green requires deterministic output. Live API responses differ between Blue's run and Green's comparison run. Idempotent Silver pipeline = blue-green.

questions.question: How does CelticWind treat rollback?
options: Safety net for bugs that escape all 4 layers (CI, staging, blue-green, monitoring) — not a planned step
First option — every deployment plans for rollback as the primary recovery path immediately on issues
Last option — every deployment is final and rollback is forbidden once production traffic flows live
Manual only — rollback requires senior engineer approval through a multi-step Lithuanian regulatory process
questions.correctAnswer: Safety net for bugs that escape all 4 layers (CI, staging, blue-green, monitoring) — not a planned step
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: rollback = safety net, not planned step. Mandatory post-mortem: why didn't tests catch this? Drives test/quality-gate/staging improvements.

questions.question: What does the deployment pipeline enable for Module 8's optimization work?
options: Tests verify correctness preserved; performance regression tests confirm improvement — safe optimization
The pipeline auto-applies optimizations through static analysis on every commit to the main branch
The pipeline blocks all optimizations until a senior engineer manually approves through a separate process
The pipeline requires optimizations to be deployed to staging for 30 days before production deployment
questions.correctAnswer: Tests verify correctness preserved; performance regression tests confirm improvement — safe optimization
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: optimize → CI verifies tests pass (correctness) → perf regression tests confirm improvement. Without CI/CD, broken optimizations reach production.

questions.question: Why is performance intuition wrong more often than not?
options: Modern systems have non-obvious bottlenecks: regex backtracking, GC pauses, per-row overhead at scale
Modern systems are too complex to understand — only Apache-certified consultants can profile correctly
Modern systems use machine learning for execution — human reasoning cannot anticipate ML decisions
Modern systems run on distributed infrastructure that hides bottlenecks behind a network abstraction
questions.correctAnswer: Modern systems have non-obvious bottlenecks: regex backtracking, GC pauses, per-row overhead at scale
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: 'Parquet compression seems expensive' = intuition wrong. CPU stuck on regex backtracking, not I/O. Profile first, never guess.

questions.question: What does cProfile's `cumtime` (cumulative time) tell you?
options: Total time spent in this function including all functions it called — top-down hierarchical view
Total time spent waiting for I/O during this function's execution — pure wall-clock measurement
Total time the function would take if run on perfectly optimized hardware in a vacuum
Total time the function spent in Python's GC during garbage collection cycles between calls
questions.correctAnswer: Total time spent in this function including all functions it called — top-down hierarchical view
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: cumtime = self + callees. tottime = self only. cProfile sorts by cumtime to find the slowest call chains.

questions.question: What is the most common Python performance anti-pattern in regex usage?
options: re.compile() called inside a per-row function instead of once at module level — 1.2M compiles wastes 60% time
re.compile() with a pattern longer than 100 characters — the regex engine cannot optimize complex patterns
re.compile() with the IGNORECASE flag — case-insensitive matching is 10× slower than case-sensitive
re.compile() inside an if statement — the regex engine must re-evaluate the pattern on every condition
questions.correctAnswer: re.compile() called inside a per-row function instead of once at module level — 1.2M compiles wastes 60% time
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: per-call compile = 60% of validation time. Module-level _PATTERN = re.compile(...) once = 84% reduction from one line change.

questions.question: What is the most useful information to log alongside an I/O timing?
options: Data size (bytes, rows) — 4.2s for 200 MB is normal; 4.2s for 20 MB is a problem only with size
Engineer name — required by Lithuanian regulations to track which developer initiated the I/O
Random nonce — to make every log entry unique for the security team's tamper-detection auditing
CPU usage percentage — I/O timings need CPU usage to be meaningful for capacity planning later
questions.correctAnswer: Data size (bytes, rows) — 4.2s for 200 MB is normal; 4.2s for 20 MB is a problem only with size
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: 4.2s/200MB = 48 MB/s (normal). 4.2s/20MB = 4.8 MB/s (problem). Without size, the timing is meaningless. Throughput = comparable across runs.

questions.question: When does an O(n²) algorithm become a problem?
options: Invisible at 100 rows (microseconds), dominates at 1.2M rows (45 seconds) — scale exposes complexity
Always — O(n²) is forbidden by Apache PEP 8 regardless of the input size or the algorithm's purpose
Never — Python's interpreter automatically rewrites O(n²) algorithms to O(n) at runtime via JIT compilation
Only with strings — O(n²) on numbers is fine because Python's number operations are vectorized
questions.correctAnswer: Invisible at 100 rows (microseconds), dominates at 1.2M rows (45 seconds) — scale exposes complexity
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: 100 rows = microseconds (invisible). 1.2M rows = 45s (dominates). Use set/dict for O(1) lookups instead of nested loops.

questions.question: What is the rule about strings in Python performance code?
options: Use ','.join() for variable-length combination; f-strings for fixed templates — never += in a loop
Use string concatenation everywhere — Python optimizes += automatically through interning at startup
Use bytes operations exclusively — strings are 5× slower than bytes for any serialization workload
Use Cython for all string code — pure Python strings are too slow for any production pipeline always
questions.correctAnswer: Use ','.join() for variable-length combination; f-strings for fixed templates — never += in a loop
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: += in loop = O(n²) immutable string copies. join = O(n). 30× speedup at 1.2M rows. f-strings for templates, join for variable lists.

questions.question: What does Parquet column projection (`columns=['turbine_id', ...]`) achieve?
options: Read only the needed columns — 6.8× faster, 4.7× less memory than reading all columns by default
Encrypt only the listed columns — Parquet's column-level encryption applies only to projected columns
Validate only the listed columns — Parquet skips schema validation for the columns not in the list
Cache only the listed columns — Parquet's read cache stores only the projected columns persistently
questions.correctAnswer: Read only the needed columns — 6.8× faster, 4.7× less memory than reading all columns by default
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: columnar storage = read what you need. CelticWind: 12-column scan all (8.2s, 450MB) vs 3-column projection (1.2s, 95MB).

questions.question: How does CelticWind tune chunk size?
options: Benchmark sizes (1K-500K) with tracemalloc — find the sweet spot between throughput and memory usage
Always 50,000 — the standard chunk size for Python pipelines per the engineering style guide
Always 1,000 — small chunks reduce memory; throughput penalty is acceptable for safety reasons
Always one row — Python's interpreter optimizes single-row operations better than batched ones at scale
questions.correctAnswer: Benchmark sizes (1K-500K) with tracemalloc — find the sweet spot between throughput and memory usage
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: 50K = sweet spot for CSV+Pydantic on 4GB server. Polars optimal at 500K (vectorized = lower per-chunk overhead). Workload-dependent.

questions.question: When should you NOT optimize?
options: Pipeline runs within 50% of SLA — the headroom accommodates data growth and infrastructure variability
Pipeline runs in development — only optimize on production servers with real workloads to measure properly
Pipeline uses Python — Python is too slow to optimize meaningfully so always rewrite in Rust instead
Pipeline serves billing — billing pipelines must never be modified per Lithuanian regulatory framework
questions.correctAnswer: Pipeline runs within 50% of SLA — the headroom accommodates data growth and infrastructure variability
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: 50% SLA headroom = safety margin for growth. Optimization beyond readability = expensive maintenance. Each optimization must include why-comment with profile data.

questions.question: What does CelticWind's performance baseline look like after Module 8 optimization?
options: Read: 96s→15s, Validate: 45s→25s (compiled regex), Total pipeline runs in 63s (was 171s)
Read: 96s→0s — Python's interpreter learned to read CSV files instantly through machine learning
Read: 96s→480s — adding profiling overhead made the pipeline slower than the original sequential one
Read: 96s→96s — optimizations were rejected because they broke the pipeline's readability metrics
questions.correctAnswer: Read: 96s→15s, Validate: 45s→25s (compiled regex), Total pipeline runs in 63s (was 171s)
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: ThreadPoolExecutor (M2) for read, compiled regex for validate. 171s → 63s. Module 9 builds observability on top of this baseline.

questions.question: What is the shift from logging to observability?
options: From answering known questions ('did it finish?') to making behavior visible enough that unexpected patterns are detectable
From writing logs to an SQL database instead of writing them to a file — pure storage backend change only
From English logs to JSON logs — observability is purely a structured-format change with no semantic shift
From local logs to remote logs — observability moves logs from the application's host to a central server
questions.correctAnswer: From answering known questions ('did it finish?') to making behavior visible enough that unexpected patterns are detectable
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: logs (anticipated questions) + metrics (unanticipated trends) + health checks (operational) = observability. Visibility for unexpected patterns.

questions.question: Why is the first argument to `logger.info` an event name (not a message)?
options: Event names are stable identifiers that log queries rely on — messages change format and break queries
Event names are encrypted with a per-event key — messages are visible to anyone reading the log file
Event names are cached by structlog for performance — messages are recomputed on every log call
Event names are required by Apache PEP 8 because they enable type-safe log query construction always
questions.correctAnswer: Event names are stable identifiers that log queries rely on — messages change format and break queries
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: 'validation_complete' = stable. 'Validation completed with 399850 valid...' = format changes break queries. Event names = API names, treated as stable.

questions.question: What does `bind_contextvars(run_id=...)` enable across pipeline functions?
options: Every subsequent log event in this run inherits run_id — propagates through asyncio + ThreadPoolExecutor automatically
Every subsequent log event is encrypted with run_id as the key — security audit log per pipeline run
Every subsequent log event is throttled by run_id — only one event per second per run reaches the log
Every subsequent log event is replicated across data centers tagged with run_id for global availability
questions.correctAnswer: Every subsequent log event in this run inherits run_id — propagates through asyncio + ThreadPoolExecutor automatically
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: contextvars propagate through async + threads (3.12+). ProcessPoolExecutor needs explicit re-binding in each worker (separate process = separate context).

questions.question: Why does CelticWind cap Prometheus label cardinality?
options: Each unique label combination = separate time series — meter_id label = 1.2M time series overwhelming storage
Cardinality is required to be below 100 by Apache's specification for any production metrics export
Cardinality affects encryption strength — high cardinality makes per-label encryption keys easier to brute force
Cardinality affects pricing — Prometheus charges per unique time series and high cardinality increases bills
questions.correctAnswer: Each unique label combination = separate time series — meter_id label = 1.2M time series overwhelming storage
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: pipeline (12) × step (5) × status (3) = ~180 series. meter_id as label = 1.2M series → Prometheus collapses. Meter-level metrics belong in logs.

questions.question: What is CelticWind's policy on metrics with no alert?
options: Either has an alert with actionable runbook OR is a diagnostic metric consulted during investigation
Every metric must have an alert — diagnostic-only metrics are forbidden by the engineering policy
No metric should have an alert — the on-call team checks Grafana every 15 minutes during business hours
Metrics without alerts must be removed from the dashboard within 30 days regardless of their utility
questions.correctAnswer: Either has an alert with actionable runbook OR is a diagnostic metric consulted during investigation
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: alert without action = noise. Diagnostic metrics (consulted during investigation) are fine. Core 5 metrics have alerts; diagnostic metrics don't.

questions.question: What is the difference between a liveness check and a readiness check in Kubernetes?
options: Liveness: process alive and responsive? Readiness: can the pipeline run successfully right now?
Liveness: every 30s, restart on 3 failures. Readiness: every 60s, ignore failures permanently
Liveness: HTTP 200 always. Readiness: HTTP 200 if the database is reachable for cache pre-population
Liveness: synchronous check. Readiness: async check that the Kubernetes scheduler ignores entirely
questions.correctAnswer: Liveness: process alive and responsive? Readiness: can the pipeline run successfully right now?
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: liveness = restart if dead. Readiness = traffic eligibility. Cap at 500ms total — file existence checks (microseconds) + lightweight API ping.

questions.question: What three things does an alert at 03:15 need to tell the on-call engineer?
options: What might be wrong, what to check first, what to do — runbook link is mandatory in PagerDuty
Who wrote the code, when they committed it, where they live — for rapid contact during the incident
How many users are affected, how much revenue is lost, when the regulator must be informed
Which Python version is running, which Linux kernel, which CPU architecture — for forensic analysis
questions.correctAnswer: What might be wrong, what to check first, what to do — runbook link is mandatory in PagerDuty
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: alert without runbook → escalates to platform team. Runbook tells the engineer the diagnosis path and remediation steps without prior context.

questions.question: What is the design principle for CelticWind's dashboards?
options: Each dashboard answers one specific question — metrics that don't help the question are excluded
Each dashboard shows every available metric — engineers learn to read walls of charts efficiently
Each dashboard mirrors the production code structure — one dashboard per Python module exactly
Each dashboard runs on a separate Grafana instance — isolation prevents cascading failures globally
questions.correctAnswer: Each dashboard answers one specific question — metrics that don't help the question are excluded
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: 'is the platform healthy?' (overview), 'what failed?' (detail), 'when will we need to optimize?' (historical). Metric-not-answering question = excluded.

questions.question: What is the policy for unused dashboards and alerts?
options: Dashboards with zero views in a month → candidate for removal; alerts with zero acks in a quarter → review
Keep everything forever — dashboards and alerts may be needed someday and removal is irreversible
Run a daily script that removes any dashboard not viewed in the last 24 hours automatically permanently
Migrate unused dashboards to cold storage on Lithuanian government cloud servers for compliance reasons
questions.correctAnswer: Dashboards with zero views in a month → candidate for removal; alerts with zero acks in a quarter → review
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: weekly ops review checks alert ack rates and dashboard view counts. False confidence is worse than no monitoring at all.

questions.question: What three observability surfaces does the CelticWind pipeline expose?
options: structlog JSON to stdout, Prometheus metrics on :9090, health checks on :8080
structlog JSON to S3, Prometheus metrics in Redis, health checks via Slack notification only
structlog text to syslog, Prometheus metrics on the Apache server, health checks through email replies
structlog binary to a database, Prometheus metrics in YAML files, health checks by manual verification
questions.correctAnswer: structlog JSON to stdout, Prometheus metrics on :9090, health checks on :8080
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: 3 surfaces. Logs (aggregator), metrics (Prometheus + Grafana + PagerDuty), health (K8s probes). Together = complete observability stack.

questions.question: What does Module 10 say takes years versus weeks?
options: Building takes weeks, deploying takes hours, operating takes years — operational discipline determines years
Building takes years, deploying takes months, operating takes weeks — most time is in initial development
All three take the same amount of time — Apache best practices balance phases at 1/3 each in time
Building takes hours, deploying takes minutes, operating takes seconds — modern tooling makes all fast
questions.correctAnswer: Building takes weeks, deploying takes hours, operating takes years — operational discipline determines years
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: idempotency, checkpointing, graceful shutdown, runbooks make those years smooth. Without discipline, day 1 works → day 90 degrades.

questions.question: What is CelticWind's idempotent write pattern?
options: Write to .tmp_date=X/, then atomically rename to date=X/ — partial writes overwritten on retry
Append to date=X/ on every run — duplicates are deduplicated by a downstream cleaner per partition
Write to date=X-{run_id}/ — every run writes to a unique directory and the consumer picks the latest
Write nothing — read replicas serve queries from the source data without producing any writes ever
questions.correctAnswer: Write to .tmp_date=X/, then atomically rename to date=X/ — partial writes overwritten on retry
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: tmp dir + rmtree + atomic rename. Code review checklist: 'Append mode is not permitted for production.' Quarantine log is the only exception.

questions.question: What is the relationship between checkpointing and idempotency?
options: Step must be idempotent before it can be checkpointed — failure between step and checkpoint = duplicates
Checkpointing replaces idempotency — checkpoints prevent re-execution so idempotency is unnecessary
Idempotency replaces checkpointing — idempotent steps can be replayed indefinitely without checkpoints
Both are equivalent — checkpointing and idempotency are two names for the same recovery mechanism
questions.correctAnswer: Step must be idempotent before it can be checkpointed — failure between step and checkpoint = duplicates
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: failure between step (append) + checkpoint save = duplicates on retry. Save checkpoint AFTER idempotent write. Safe resume point.

questions.question: What does graceful shutdown on SIGTERM achieve?
options: Finish the current chunk, save checkpoint, exit cleanly — Kubernetes can restart with no data corruption
Crash immediately to free resources — Kubernetes restarts the pod and resumes from a clean state
Block forever until all data is processed — SIGTERM is ignored to prevent partial output corruption
Switch to async mode — SIGTERM transitions the synchronous pipeline to coroutine-based execution
questions.correctAnswer: Finish the current chunk, save checkpoint, exit cleanly — Kubernetes can restart with no data corruption
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: shutdown.should_stop check at top of each chunk loop. break out cleanly after current chunk + checkpoint. Tested in CI integration suite.

questions.question: What three questions does a good post-mortem answer?
options: What happened (timeline), why (root cause not 'API down' but 'no fallback'), what prevents recurrence
Who failed (engineer name), when (timestamp), how to punish (HR action) — accountability-focused
Cost (revenue lost), impact (users affected), penalty (regulatory fines) — financial-focused only
Pythonic style (PEP 8), test coverage (>90%), documentation completeness (100%) — quality metrics
questions.correctAnswer: What happened (timeline), why (root cause not 'API down' but 'no fallback'), what prevents recurrence
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: blameless. Focus on systemic causes + process improvements. Post-mortem archive = institutional memory for operational failures.

questions.question: When is the best time to write a runbook?
options: Immediately after resolving the incident — diagnostic steps and fix commands are still fresh
On the engineer's birthday — to commemorate their incident response with a permanent artifact
At pipeline launch — write every possible runbook before any incident can possibly occur
Never — runbooks are obsolete the moment they are written and create false confidence in operations
questions.correctAnswer: Immediately after resolving the incident — diagnostic steps and fix commands are still fresh
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: post-mortem template includes 'if this recurs, what steps should the on-call engineer follow?' Answer = runbook. 15 min vs 1 hour from memory.

questions.question: What is the prerequisite for any reprocessing strategy?
options: Idempotent writes — without them, reprocessing produces duplicates or partial output silently
Manual approval from the regulator — Lithuanian law requires per-reprocessing authorization always
Triple-region replication — reprocessing without replicas can corrupt the only data copy permanently
questions.correctAnswer: Idempotent writes — without them, reprocessing produces duplicates or partial output silently
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: append-mode pipelines + reprocessing = duplicates. Runbook step 1: 'Verify pipeline uses idempotent writes.' If not, escalate.

questions.question: How are SLAs negotiated at CelticWind?
options: Quarterly planning meetings between engineering and each consuming team — reflects actual deadlines
Engineering sets SLAs unilaterally — consumers must adapt to whatever the platform offers each quarter
Consumers set SLAs unilaterally — engineering must meet whatever deadlines downstream teams demand
SLAs are auto-generated from past performance — no negotiation, just statistical extrapolation
questions.correctAnswer: Quarterly planning meetings between engineering and each consuming team — reflects actual deadlines
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: 06:00 SLA meaningless if billing needs 04:00. Quarterly negotiation. Billing platinum (06:00, 99.9%) reflects invoicing deadline; analytics gold (09:00, 99%).

questions.question: What does the capacity projection model produce?
options: Projected runtime per year + SLA headroom + action_needed flag (when headroom < 20%)
Projected revenue per year + cost per query + ROI flag (when payback period exceeds 2 years)
Projected team size + Python version per year + migration flag (when version EOL approaches)
Projected PR count + CI time + scaling flag (when GitHub Actions monthly minutes exceed budget)
questions.correctAnswer: Projected runtime per year + SLA headroom + action_needed flag (when headroom < 20%)
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: synthesis of every module's data — growth rate (M9 metrics), runtime (M9 gauge), bottleneck (M8 profile), parallelism (M2), memory (M3).

questions.question: What does the senior track shift from?
options: From operating systems (mid) to designing platforms that other teams build on (senior)
From writing pipelines (mid) to writing pipelines but in Rust (senior) for performance gains
From Python (mid) to Scala (senior) because senior tracks all standardize on the JVM ecosystem
From reading code (mid) to reviewing code (senior) — senior is purely a code review specialization
questions.correctAnswer: From operating systems (mid) to designing platforms that other teams build on (senior)
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: junior built pipelines, mid operated them. Senior designs platforms. Operational discipline (idempotency, monitoring, runbooks) remains the foundation.
